requirements_conformance.evidence: New file agents/query/test_intent_refiner_types.py adds 6 non-overlapping fixture queries (3 factual_lookup, 3 broad_exploratory, distinct domains from 4.3.1's 2 fixtures) plus a direct differentiation assertion (test_query_type_differentiates_categories). This literally satisfies the acceptance criteria's text ('differentiates ... verified across multiple fixture queries') and the test spec ('mocked LLM responses covering each category'). Comment: because refine_intent() performs zero classification logic of its own (it only parses/validates whatever query_type string the mock LLM returns), every one of these 6 tests is guaranteed to pass by construction -- the mock is told to return exactly the expected_type, and the assertion checks that the same value comes back out. No fixture query's *text* is ever inspected by production code, so the 'differentiation' being verified is JSON-parsing fidelity (does a string survive a round trip through json.loads and dataclass construction), not intent classification accuracy. This is a valid, minimal reading of the acceptance criteria (the topic-selector only cares that whatever the LLM says is threaded through uncorrupted) but it does not test what a naive reading of 'correctly differentiates ... categories' would suggest, i.e. that HiveMind's own logic can tell a factual query from an exploratory one.

architecture_conformance.evidence: intent_refiner.py is untouched (git diff confirms zero production-code changes). Test uses the same LLMClient ABC-subclass mocking pattern as 4.3.1's file, correct import of query.intent_refiner.refine_intent/IntentRefinerResult/QueryType, no new coupling introduced.

regression_risk.evidence: Purely additive test-only file, no shared conftest/fixtures introduced. cd agents && python3 -m pytest query/ -q: 19 passed. cd agents && python3 -m pytest . --ignore=ingestion/test_e2e_smoke.py -q: 230 passed, 2 failed -- both failures are ingestion/test_shortlist.py protobuf gencode/runtime VersionError, pre-existing, tracked under issue #46, unrelated to and unaffected by this diff (independently reproduced, matches self-consistency.json's self-report).

edge_cases.evidence: Covers 3 distinct queries per category across different domains (geography/literature/history for factual_lookup; history/business/science for broad_exploratory), better breadth than 4.3.1's single fixture per category. Does not add any new edge case beyond what 4.3.1 already covers (malformed JSON, missing fields, invalid enum value) -- correctly so, since that is explicitly out of scope per this file's own docstring.

security.evidence: No new production code, no secrets, test-only file.

performance.evidence: In-memory mocked LLM calls only; negligible runtime (19 tests in query/ run in 0.01s).

maintainability.evidence: Clear docstring explicitly scoping this file relative to 4.3.1's sibling file, avoiding ambiguity about ownership/overlap. Comment: _FakeLLMClient is duplicated verbatim (with only cosmetic differences, e.g. dropped error-path support) across test_intent_refiner.py and test_intent_refiner_types.py; a shared conftest.py fixture would reduce duplication risk if a third query/ test file is ever added. Low severity, non-blocking.

test_coverage.evidence: query/ suite: 19/19 passed (13 from 4.3.1 + 6 new here). ruff check agents/query/: clean. Coverage of the *stated* acceptance criteria is complete. Coverage of what a naive reader might expect ('the system can tell these query types apart') is architecturally impossible for a unit test of refine_intent() as designed, since classification is entirely delegated to the external LLM and never exercised for real -- see requirements_conformance comment.

confidence.evidence: Independently re-ran both pytest suites and ruff rather than trusting the commit message / self-consistency.json's self-report; all figures matched. The one open question (architectural sufficiency of a parse-and-validate design against a 'differentiates categories' acceptance criterion) is a judgment call disclosed explicitly below rather than a verification gap.

architectural_gap_analysis.question: Does 4.3.2's acceptance criteria, as literally written, imply real classification/heuristic logic that is structurally absent from 4.3.1's refine_intent()?
architectural_gap_analysis.conclusion: No hard architectural violation, but a real interpretive tension exists. refine_intent() is, by 4.3.1's own explicit design (disclosed in its module docstring and independently confirmed PASS_WITH_COMMENTS by prior verification), a pure prompt-then-parse-JSON function: it delegates all classification to the LLM and only validates the returned query_type string against a closed 2-value enum. Under that design, 'the refiner correctly differentiates ... query_type categories ... verified across multiple fixture queries' can only ever mean 'the refiner does not corrupt or collapse whatever category the LLM reports, across several representative query shapes' -- there is no other classification behavior in this module to test. That is a legitimate, minimal, and defensible reading (and matches the LLD, which specifies the field but not a client-side classification algorithm). It is NOT the only plausible reading: a reader could reasonably expect 4.3.2's tests to prove the *system* (LLM + refiner together, or refiner with fallback heuristics) reliably classifies real query text -- something no mocked-LLM unit test can ever prove regardless of how it's written, since the mock is told the answer in advance. This is a design-time gap that belongs to 4.3.1 (or an unstated integration-test subtask), not a defect introduced by 4.3.2. Because 4.3.1 already passed CDR review with this design accepted and disclosed, 4.3.2 cannot retroactively be held to a stricter architectural standard than its own dependency was verified against. Recommendation: PASS_WITH_COMMENTS rather than CHANGES_REQUESTED, with the gap flagged as a forward-looking note for whoever eventually verifies topic_selector.py or any future subtask claiming end-to-end classification accuracy against real (unmocked) query text -- that is where an integration/eval-style test would need to live.

findings_non_blocking: Acceptance criteria 'correctly differentiates' is satisfied only in the minimal parse-fidelity sense, not in a classification-accuracy sense, because refine_intent() has no classification logic of its own -- see architectural_gap_analysis. Not a defect in this commit; a disclosed limitation inherited from 4.3.1's design.
_FakeLLMClient duplicated near-verbatim across test_intent_refiner.py and test_intent_refiner_types.py; consider a shared conftest.py fixture if a third file in agents/query/ needs the same fake.
No new edge cases added beyond 4.3.1's; correctly out of scope for this subtask.

issue_22_closeout_assessment.rationale: 4.3.1 was independently verified PASS_WITH_COMMENTS (run 012-verification, commit 694b0e3) with disclosed, non-blocking findings (F-4.3.1-1/2/3, all low/info severity). 4.3.2 (this run, commit 08f434e) is additive, test-only, does not touch production code, passes its own test spec verbatim, and does not introduce any new regression. Both subtasks' non-blocking findings are documentation/lint/coverage-breadth items, not defects that block functionality the topic-selector will depend on. The one substantive open question (architectural sufficiency of a parse-and-validate design against a 'differentiates categories' acceptance criterion) is a forward-looking design note for the *next* subtask (topic_selector.py, not yet started) rather than a blocker for closing issue #22 as scoped. Recommend closing issue #22 with both findings sets carried forward as regression-index entries for whoever picks up topic_selector.py.
